source_title: Manual de Periodismo de Datos Iberoamericano
author: Felipe Perry, Miguel Paz (eds.)

extracts.extract: The work of collecting, processing, and delivering data in adverse circumstances has produced a form of learning that should keep us alert so as not to fall into the trap of easy shortcuts when processing data and creating visualization formats. Rigor, an obsession with details, and the determination to tackle issues of social impact are elements that will enable the generation of relevant, well-told, and well-documented stories.
extracts.notes: From editors' introduction; framed around Latin American context of limited data access

extracts.extract: Philip Meyer, tired of newspapers being filled with politician interviews and official press releases, decided he would create his own source by building a database. He investigated campaign financing of board members and demonstrated that 65 percent of the campaign contributions originated from insurance companies. This marks the origins of precision journalism and its sociological research methods as the precursor to data journalism.
extracts.notes: Historical precedent from Philip Meyer at Miami Herald (1959)
